title: Chapter 2 (intermediate) — Data, rendering & synthesis
meta.desc: Intermediate level: data-driven biomes, a renderer-agnostic visuals module, runtime-generated textures, synthesized Web Audio, parallax via depth/scrollFactor, and quality tiers from a startup benchmark.
hero.title: Data, rendering & synthesis
hero.lead: How a single game wears four very different outfits: biomes described as data, a renderer-agnostic visuals module, textures and sound generated at runtime, depth through layers, and quality that adapts to the device.
s1.lead: A biome isn't code: it's data. One plain object describes the sky, the terrain palette, the celestial body, the parallax ridges and the ambient particle effect. The renderer reads the sheet and paints the rest.
s1.p1: Four biomes — Meadow 🌳, Desert 🏜️, Tundra ❄️, Volcano 🌋 — share one engine. To add a fifth, you add an object, not a code path.
s1.callout.tip: Why data, not code. Settings that live as data are easy to tweak, test and serialize. Notice roughness rides along in the biome: the same number drives the terrain generator and the wind feel, keeping each place coherent.
s2.h2: One renderer-agnostic module for two surfaces
s2.p1: The TV draws with Phaser; the phone controller draws a small preview on a plain canvas. To keep the cannon, the charge tint and the windsock identical on both, the shared maths lives in src/render/visuals.js — pure functions that return numbers, never touching either renderer.
s2.code.c1: // barrel: cool steel at low power, glowing red at full charge
s2.p2: This module knows neither Phaser nor the DOM: it returns numbers (colours, geometry points). Each view draws them with its own technology — one shared source of truth for the look.
s2.callout.nerd: For the curious. The module exposes helpers like shade(), craterRimColor() and computeWindsock(). None imports Phaser. That's the rule: anything shared between renderers must stay renderer-agnostic — it computes geometry and colours, and each surface draws them its own way.
s3.p1: The project ships no image files. The particle sprites — spark, smoke, flash, cloud — are drawn once at boot into off-screen textures, then tinted and blended at runtime. Asset-free, yet fully styleable.
s3.p2: No audio files either. Every effect is built at runtime from oscillators and filtered noise through the Web Audio API, in src/systems/Sfx.js. A cannon shot, for instance, is a short low-passed noise crack plus a descending sine thump.
s3.callout.audiogate: The browser gate. Browsers start the audio context suspended; it can only resume after a user gesture. So Sfx.unlock() runs on the first tap/click — without it, the first sounds would silently never play.
s3.callout.tip: Remember. An “asset-free” bundle is tiny, caches trivially, and suffers no blocking load. The cost shifts to CPU at runtime — a trade-off we accept (see the next section).
s4.p1: The fake 3D depth is just two Phaser properties per layer: depth (draw order) and scrollFactor (how much it follows the camera). Far ridges scroll slowly, the foreground scrolls fully — and the inter-round camera pan reveals continuous parallax.
s4.p2: Smart TVs and weak browsers can choke on the full particle/parallax load. So the host measures its own frame rate for a short window at startup and records a quality tier. The renderer then scales effects to match.
s4.code2.cap: src/systems/benchmark.js — pick a tier from real fps
s4.callout.nerd: Remember. Batch 2's architecture is “describe, don't hard-code”: biomes as data, visuals as shared pure functions, textures and sound synthesized on the fly — and an honest measurement deciding how much to draw.
